coredata.dc:title: Application of deep eutectic solvent-based extraction coupled with an S-CQD fluorescent sensor for the determination of pirimicarb in cereals
coredata.dc:creator: Jing, Xu
Wu, Jing
Wang, Huihui
Guo, Luyao
Zheng, Xiaojiao
Wang, Xiaowen
Wang, Shuo
coredata.dc:description: A novel deep eutectic solvent-based extraction and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) serving as fluorescence probes to detect pirimicarb in cereals were established. The deep eutectic solvent was synthesized using choline chloride and butanediol, achieving direct and efficient extraction of pirimicarb residue in the cereals. The fluorescence quenching of S-CQDs was caused by the electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged S-CQDs and positively charged thiocholine, which was the hydrolysate of acetylthiocholine. The fluorescence of S-CQDs was enhanced as the activity of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) was inhibited by pirimicarb, achieving the detection of pirimicarb in the cereal samples. The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.006 μg mL−1. The recovery ranged from 96.6% to 108.2%. This extraction and detection method of pirimicarb based on an environmentally friendly DES and S-CQD fluorescent sensor maintains good stability and convenience, offering a promising strategy for extracting and testing harmful substances in food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pirimicarb
Deep eutectic solvent
Carbon quantum dot
Fluorescence
Cereal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots
Characterization of sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots
The percentage of water in DES
Application of the proposed method in cereal samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimization of the extraction conditions (a: the effect of different types of polyhydric alcohol as HBDs on the recovery; b: the effect of different volume of DES on the recovery; c: the effect of different percentages of water in DES on the recovery; d: the effect of different extraction time on the recovery).

Optimization of the detection conditions (a: the effect of different concentrations of AChE on the fluorescence intensity; b: the effect of different pH values on the fluorescence intensity; c: the effect of different concentrations of ATC on the fluorescence intensity; d: the effect of different reaction time on the fluorescence intensity).
The effect of different types of insecticides on fluorescence intensity.
Determination of pirimicarb in different kinds of cereal samples.

head.ce:title: Application of deep eutectic solvent-based extraction coupled with an S-CQD fluorescent sensor for the determination of pirimicarb in cereals

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Shanxi Agricultural University, Taigu, Shanxi 030801, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Shanxi Agricultural University, Taigu, Shanxi 030801, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Science and Health, School of Medicine, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Science and Health

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Science and Health, School of Medicine, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel deep eutectic solvent-based extraction and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) serving as fluorescence probes to detect pirimicarb in cereals were established. The deep eutectic solvent was synthesized using choline chloride and butanediol, achieving direct and efficient extraction of pirimicarb residue in the cereals. The fluorescence quenching of S-CQDs was caused by the electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged S-CQDs and positively charged thiocholine, which was the hydrolysate of acetylthiocholine. The fluorescence of S-CQDs was enhanced as the activity of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) was inhibited by pirimicarb, achieving the detection of pirimicarb in the cereal samples. The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.006 μg mL−1. The recovery ranged from 96.6% to 108.2%. This extraction and detection method of pirimicarb based on an environmentally friendly DES and S-CQD fluorescent sensor maintains good stability and convenience, offering a promising strategy for extracting and testing harmful substances in food samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Pirimicarb, a pyrimidine carbamate insecticide with high activity, is usually used as an aphicide in agriculture, especially for organophosphorus-resistant aphids (Champagne, Kumar, & Ling, 2019). However, pirimicarb may cause potential risks, such as residual pesticides in agricultural products, soil, and water environments due to its extensive and improper use in agriculture, impacting human health. Pirimicarb, a kind of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibitor, enters the human body through the food chain, causing the accumulation of acetylcholine neurotransmitter, which induces headache, convulsion, paralysis, neurotoxicity, cancerization, and even death (Apilux, Siangproh, Insin, Chailapakul, & Prachayasittikul, 2017; Nunes, Korn, & Lemos, 2021; Zhou et al., 2010). To date, numerous efforts have been made to develop various techniques for the quantification of pirimicarb, including high-performance liquid chromatography (Ulusoy, Koseoglu, Kabir, Ulusoy, & Locatelli, 2020), gas chromatography (Shen, Su, Zhu, & Gao, 2007), mass spectrometry (Choi, Ng, Hu, Li, & Yao, 2020), and capillary electrochromatography (Juan-García, Font, & Picó, 2005). Although the aforementioned approaches prove to have good selectivity and sensitivity, these assays also have demerits, including expensive instruments, complicated operations, and the need for skilled operators. There are significant practical benefits to establish a novel approach for extracting and detecting pirimicarb with the merits of being low-cost, simple, efficient, and environmentally friendly.

Fluorescent sensors have been broadly developed to detect trace substances due to their economy, simplicity, high sensitivity, and multiplexed detection capacity. To date, various fluorescent materials have been exploited, including organic dyes, noble-metal (gold, silver, and copper) nanoclusters, quantum dots, and others (Han et al., 2019). Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as novel nanomaterials in the quantum dot family, have drawn increasing attention due to their advantages of good fluorescence stability, high aqueous solubility, biocompatibility, and low toxicity (Yang et al., 2020). In addition, the fluorescence properties of the CQDs can be easily adjusted by controlling the reaction precursors and reaction conditions during the synthesis process (Dong et al., 2020). Doping heteroatoms with different electronegativities into CQDs can effectively adjust the bandgap and electron density, contributing to changes in the electronic, chemical, and optical properties (Kou, Jiang, Park, & Meng, 2020). The doping heteroatoms of CQDs have huge application prospects in the field of food safety due to their high fluorescence intensity with size tunability, excellent photobleaching resistance, and symmetrical and narrow tunable emission spectra (Chen et al., 2020).

Sample pretreatment is the crucial step for the enrichment of the analyte and elimination of the matrix effect before determination (Jing et al., 2021; Locateni et al., 2019). Toxic acetonitrile and methanol have been widely used as extractants to separate target analytes from solid food samples in the past several decades (Musarurwa, Chimuka, & Tavengwa, 2019). In contrast, deep eutectic solvents (DESs), as green and nontoxic solvents, provide a valuable alternative to overcome the limitations of conventional organic solvents (Jouyban, Farajzadeh, & Afshar Mogaddam, 2020). DES is commonly synthesized using a hydrogen bond acceptor (HBA) and a hydrogen bond donor (HBD) in a specific molar ratio at an appropriate temperature. Due to the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds, the melting points of DES are lower than those of individual components (Paetzold et al., 2019). DESs, which have virtues, including simple preparation, chemical stability, and biodegradation, are considered outstanding alternative extractants in the field of food analysis (Shishov et al., 2019).

Herein, a simple pretreatment and fluorescence sensing analysis strategy of pirimicarb was developed. In this study, green DES as the extractant was synthesized using choline chloride and butanediol to efficiently extract target analyte from complex matrix samples replacing traditional toxic organic solvents. Sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) as fluorescent probes were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using sodium sulfide and citric acid (Wang et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2020). The synthesized S-CQDs processed abundant negative charges on their surface with blue-green fluorescence. AChE could catalyze the hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine (ATC) into thiocholine (TC) as the fluorescence quencher, which processed positive charges. The fluorescence quenching of S-CQDs was caused based on the electrostatic interaction between S-CQDs and TC. The fluorescence of the system was enhanced due to pirimicarb inhibiting the activity of AChE, thus achieving the quantitative detection of residual pirimicarb in cereal samples (Fig. 1 ). The potential applications of this simple, economical, and environmentally friendly method were demonstrated by detecting the pirimicarb content in cereal samples.

Pirimicarb (99%), choline chloride (ChCl), ethylene glycol, 1,3-propanediol, 1,4-butanediol, acetylcholinesterase from electric eel (AChE, 200 U g−1), acetyl thiocholine iodide, chlorantraniliprole, fipronil, abamectin, cyhalothrin, imidacloprid, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, disodium hydrogen phosphate, citric acid, sodium sulfide, and methanol were all analytical grades and obtained from Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cereal samples (rice, wheat, millet, and oat) were acquired from a local supermarket (Taigu, China).

In this study, DES was synthesized by mixing ChCl serving as HBA and butanediol as HBD in a molar ratio of 1:1 at 90 °C until a homogeneous liquid was formed (Mahmood, Lorwirachsutee, Theodoropoulos, & Gonzalez-Miquel, 2019). The DES was used in the subsequent extraction procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: S-CQDs were synthesized by the one-step hydrothermal method based on the slightly modified method of the literature (Yang et al., 2020). First, 0.400 g of citric acid was dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water under stirring. Then, 0.113 g of sodium sulfide was added to the as-prepared citric acid solution and stirred for 10 min. The mixture was poured and sealed into a 50 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and hydrothermally carbonized at 190℃ for 12 h. The synthesized quantum dots were preserved at 4 °C in a refrigerator for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size of as-prepared S-CQDs fluorescent probe were carried out by transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30) at 200 kV. The Zeta potential was detected on Zeta sizer Nano ZS9. The Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was recorded on the Nicolet IS10 FTIR spectrometer in a range of 400–4000 cm−1. The detailed analyses of the characterization results are present in Supplementary Material.

In this work, 1 g of cereal powder sample (rice, wheat, millet, or oat) was placed into a 5-mL centrifuge tube. Then 1 mL of DES (ChCl: butanediol) was transferred into the cereal sample. Then, the tube was vortexed for 15 s at 2500 r min−1 to transfer the target analyte to the DES. Finally, the extracted solution was filtered through a 0.22-μm filter before detection.

Next, 100 μL of the above filtrate was placed in a well of 96-well plates, and then 50 μL of AChE (50 mU mL−1) was added to the well and left at 37 °C for 50 min, ensuring full inhibition of the activity of AChE. Then 50 μL of ATC (2 mmoL L−1), 50 μL of sodium dihydrogen phosphate-disodium hydrogen phosphate buffer (pH = 7.0), and 50 μL of the above synthesized S-CQDs were added to the well and incubated at 37 °C for 40 min. Fluorescence readings were performed by a SpectraMax i3x microplate reader (Molecular Devices, China). The excitation and emission wavelengths were 325 and 395 nm, respectively.

The recovery was utilized to evaluate the extraction performance of the proposed method according to the following equation:
Recovery
\%
=
C
found
-
C
real
C
added
× 100
\%
C found indicates the concentration of pirimicarb obtained after adding the known amount of pirimicarb in the sample, C real represents the original concentration of pirimicarb in the sample, and C added denotes the concentration of pirimicarb added to the sample.

It is the key to select the appropriate DES to efficiently extract the target pesticides from the complex sample matrix (Zhao, Meng, Zhao, & Zhao, 2020). In the current study, three different DESs were prepared using ChCl as HBA and polyhydric alcohol (ethylene glycol, 1,3-propanediol, or 1,4-butanediol) as HBD in a molar ratio of 1:1, investigating the extraction effect of different DESs on the target analyte. The result is shown in Fig. 2 a. The DES synthesized by ChCl and butanediol possessed the highest recovery. This might be because the DES (ChCl: butanediol) had weaker polarity than other DESs. Polarity is an important property of DESs as the extractant to be considered (Tang & Row, 2020). Perhaps the space structure of the DES was more suitable for the extraction of pirimicarb. Therefore, DES formed by ChCl and butanediol was selected as the extractant for the subsequent experiments.

The volume of the DES influences the extraction efficiency of the analyte (Jia, Huang, Zhao, Wang, & Jing, 2020). Different volumes of DES (ChCl: butanediol) were executed to evaluate the extraction performance. The result is shown in Fig. 2b. The recovery increased with the volume of DES from 750 to 1000 μL and then plateaued at volumes exceeding 1000 μL. The target analyte was not completely extracted into DES, which was in a smaller volume. Nevertheless, further increasing the volume of the DES could reduce the enrichment factor and sensitivity. Thus, 1000 μL was chosen as the optimum volume.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The percentage of water in DES
ce:section.ce:para: Water was added to DESs to fine-tune their physicochemical properties (mainly viscosity) for better extraction performance (El Achkar, Greige-Gerges, & Fourmentin, 2021). The effect of the percentage of water in the DES on the recovery was investigated. The result in Fig. 2c suggested that the recovery gradually decreased as the percentage of water in the DES increased. Thus, the synthesized DES was directly used to extract the analyte without additional water.

An efficient vortex could accelerate analyte transfer to the DES (Faraji, 2019). The vortex time of extraction was tested to evaluate the performance on the recovery. The result (Fig. 2d) showed that the recovery significantly increased with the vortex time in 0–15 s, and then the recovery decreased with the extension of vortex time exceeding 15 s. Thus, 15 s was chosen as the optimal vortex time for extraction.

The optimum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of the synthesized S-CQDs with blue-green fluorescence were 325 nm and 395 nm, respectively (Fig. S1). To verify the feasibility of this study, comparative tests of two different reaction systems (a: AChE + ATC + buffer (pH = 7) + S-CQDs) and b: pirimicarb + AChE + ATC + buffer (pH = 7) + S-CQDs) were conducted. The fluorescence spectra measured at the optimal excitation wavelength are shown in Fig. S2. The fluorescence was significantly enhanced when pirimicarb existed in the reaction system. The detection mechanism are as following. The Zeta potential of the synthesized S-CQDs was negative (Fig. S4). This was because the attachment of abundant OH− and COO− on the surface of the S-CQDs (Fig. S5) (Wang et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2020). The positively charged TC was the hydrolysate of ATC with the catalysis of AChE (Liang & Han, 2020; Zhang et al., 2014). Thus, the cause of the fluorescence quenching was the aggregation of S-CQDs because TC adsorbed the negatively charged S-CQDs through the electrostatic interaction. The aggregation of S-CQDs would lead to a decrease in the fuorescence intensity, which relied on the catalytic activity of AChE. However, when pirimicarb existed in the reaction system, the activity of AChE could be inhibited. Thus, the content of TC was reduced, and the aggregation amount of S-CQDs was decreased, leading to enhanced fluorescence. Therefore, the quantitative detection of pirimicarb in cereal samples could be realized by measuring the fluorescence of S-CQDs.

AChE could catalyze the hydrolysis of ATC into TC with positive charges as the fluorescence quencher. The effect of different concentrations of AChE (0.1, 1, 5, 50, 100, and 200 mU mL−1) on the fluorescence intensity was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased within the range from 0.1 to 50 mU mL−1 and then increased when the concentration exceeded 50 mU mL−1. Therefore, the concentration of AChE was selected as 50 mU mL−1.

At a suitable pH, the enzyme activity reached a maximum. In this work, the effect of different pH buffers (5.8, 6.2, 6.6, 7.0, 7.4, and 7.8) on AChE activity was elucidated. Sodium dihydrogen phosphate (0.2 mmol L−1) and disodium hydrogen phosphate (0.2 mmol L−1) were used to prepare different pH buffers. The results (Fig. 3b) confirmed that the fluorescence intensity was the lowest when the buffer pH was 7.0 because the AChE activity was maximum at the pH of 7.0. Therefore, the optimal pH for the AChE was 7.0.

ATC is hydrolyzed by AChE to positively charged TC as a fluorescence quencher. The influence of the ATC concentration (50 μmol L−1, 100 μmol L−1, 500 μmol L−1, 2 mmol L−1, 5 mmol L−1, 10 mmol L−1) on the fluorescence intensity was investigated. The results (Fig. 3c) indicated that the fluorescence intensity decreased with increasing concentrations of ATCs ranging from 50 μmol L−1 to 2 mmol L−1. The quenching of S-CQDS caused by electrostatic interactions between S-CQDS and TC was gradually enhanced, reducing the fluorescence intensity. However, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased as the concentration exceeded 2 mmoL L−1. Therefore, 2 mmoL L−1 ATC was considered the optimal concentration.

To guarantee the adequate electrostatic interaction between S-CQDS and TC, the reaction time was investigated experimentally. The results (Fig. 3d) suggested that the fluorescence intensity continuously decreased and then tended to be stable after 40 min, suggesting that 40 min was adequate.

To fully inhibit the activity of AChE by pirimicarb, the incubation time of AChE and the extracted solution was investigated. The result is shown in Fig. S6. The fluorescence intensity increased as the incubation time was increased from 10 to 50 min. The activity of AChE was fully inhibited with the extension of the incubation time. As the TC amount was reduced, the fluorescence intensity was increased. The fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with the extension of the incubation time after 50 min. Excessive incubation time at the optimal temperature led to a decrease in AChE activity. Therefore, 50 min was selected as the most suitable incubation time of AChE and extracted solution.

To evaluate the quantification performance of the proposed method, a series of parameters concerning the linear range, coefficient of determination (R 2), the limit of detection (LOD), and limit of quantification (LOQ) were investigated under the optimal assay conditions. The calculations for LOD and LOQ were based on the equations LOD = 3σ K−1 and LOQ = 10σ K−1 (σ was the standard deviation of 10 blank sample replicates; K was the slope of the calibration standard curve). The results (Fig. 4 ) suggested that a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of pirimicarb working standard solution within the range from 0.022 to 5 μg mL−1 was constructed. The linear equation could be put as y=(13.1274x + 4.9666) × 107, R 2 = 0.9960. The LOD and LOQ were calculated to be 0.006 and 0.022 μg mL−1, respectively. These results suggested the good sensitivity and wide detection range of the pirimicarb detection.

The anti-interference performance of the proposed method for detection of pirimicarb was investigated by comparison with other insecticides at same concentration (1 μg mL−1). As shown in Fig. 5 , the addition of pirimicarb resulted to the strong fluorescence signal. Other insecticides (chlorantraniliprole, fipronil, abamectin, cyhalothrin, and imidacloprid) showed negligible influences on the detection of pirimicarb, suggesting the favorable anti-interference performance of the fluorescent sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the proposed method in cereal samples
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed method and standard method (GB 23200.9–2016) were used to detect pirimicarb in cereal samples (rice, wheat, millet, and oat) to demonstrate the potential applications. The preliminary study found that the residual content of pirimicarb in these samples was lower than the LOD of these methods. Therefore, the accuracy was expressed as spiked recovery by analyzing blank samples spiked with different known concentrations of standard pirimicarb (0.05, 0.5, and 5 mg kg−1). The precision was evaluated by the relative standard deviation (RSD). The results are shown in Table 1 . The spiked recoveries ranged from 96.6% to 108.2%, with RSD < 5%, indicating that the method was accurate and reliable. T-values indicate that the spiked recovery was insignificantly different between both methods at the 95% confidence level. This demonstrated that the extraction method and sensor constructed in this study were trustworthy for quantitation and monitoring the level of pirimicarb in real cereal samples.

In this paper, DES-based extraction combined with an S-CQD fluorescent sensor was confirmed as a novel analytical platform with feasibility and efficiency for detection of pirimicarb in cereals. An environmentally friendly DES was synthesized with ChCl and butanediol. The DES could achieve efficient and quick extraction of the target analyte and solubilization with a fluorescent aqueous system, avoiding the use of toxic organic extractants. The S-CQDs, as fluorescent probes with abundant negative charges, were synthesized from citric acid and sodium sulfide using a simple green one-step hydrothermal method. AChE could catalyze the hydrolysis of ATC into TC with positive charges, which interacted with S-CQDs and served as fluorescent quencher leading to the quenching of the fluorescence. However, pirimicarb inhibited the activity of AChE, which enhanced the fluorescence of the system. The practicability was proven by employing the method to detect the content of pirimicarb in different cereal samples. The method was cost-efficient, easy to operate, and environmentally friendly and could accommodate the requirements of pirimicarb determination. It is anticipated that the extraction and detection method based on the DESs and S-CQD fluorescent sensor could be considered as an alternative, highly sensitive, and selective strategy in the field of food safety.

Xu Jing: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Jing Wu: Investigation, Software, Validation. Huihui Wang: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Luyao Guo: Formal analysis, Software, Data curation. Xiaojiao Zheng: Validation. Xiaowen Wang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Supervision. Shuo Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by Shanxi Province Key R&D Plan [grant number 201903D211006]; Shanxi Agricultural University Science and Technology Innovation Fund [grant number 2017YJ35].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.131360.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
